source_article.title_en: Fantasy Baseball Week 9 Preview: Top 10 sleeper pitchers feature Ryne Nelson, Walbert Urena
source_article.author: Scott White
source_article.dek_en: Two-start pitchers for Fantasy Week 8, with rankings, matchups and top streaming options
source_article.body_en:
Streaming pitchers isn't for the faint of heart, but if you find yourself without enough reliable options in a given scoring period, it may be what you need to stay afloat. Scott White has 10 recommendations for the upcoming scoring period, all rostered in less than 80 percent of CBS Sports leagues. Generally, only the top two or three are full-throated endorsements, while the rest are more about making the best of a bad situation.

All information is up to date as of late Sunday.

1. Ryne Nelson (SP) - A black sheep start against the Blue Jays on April 19 was followed by an unfair one in the thin air of Mexico City, inflating Ryne Nelson's ERA and cratering his roster rate. But he's looked great since then and has some particularly favorable matchups here against the bottom-ranked Giants and the Rockies away from Coors Field.
2. Walbert Urena (SP) - Walbert Urena excels at pitching to contact with a high ground-ball rate and basement-level exit velocity readings, and he also has some modest strikeout appeal thanks to a plus changeup. His outings have tended to run short, but he's in line for two of them week, with one being a particularly favorable matchup against the Rangers.
3. Reid Detmers (SP) - Reid Detmers continues to underperform his peripherals and has become an exhausting inclusion here week after week, but with two starts against strikeout-prone teams, you could hope for at least one quality start and upward of 15 strikeouts.
4. Andrew Abbott (SP) - Fantasy Baseballers were by and large too quick to abandon Andrew Abbott when he got off to a poor start, and now he's sporting a 1.19 ERA in his past four. The Phillies offense has shown some signs of coming around, but it still ranks in the bottom half of the league.
5. Cade Cavalli (SP) - Injuries have brought the Mets offense to a state of abject misery, ranking fourth-to-last in runs scored, and Cade Cavalli has already proven his mettle against them, striking out 10 in a quality start on April 29.
6. Bailey Ober (SP) - We're getting into more dangerous territory here, seeing as a pitch-to-contact fly-ball guy is inherently vulnerable at Fenway Park. But the Red Sox have fallen to second-to-last in runs scored, and Bailey Ober is only a start removed from a complete game shutout (otherwise known as, um, a shutout).
7. Zac Gallen (SP) - There may be no redeeming Zac Gallen in the long run, but in the short run, he lines up for two of the best matchups you could ask for, with the Giants ranking dead last in runs scored and the Rockies doing what they do away from Coors Field. In points leagues, he's a valid play.
8. Andrew Painter (SP) - The downside risk remains for Andrew Painter, but he did seem to be on the right track by fading his fastball in his latest start and ended up allowing just one run in five innings. The Reds and Guardians make for some nice matchups.
9. Eduardo Rodriguez (SP) - Eduardo Rodriguez got through his weekend start at Coors Field OK and had allowed one earned run over 15 1/3 innings in his previous two. I don't entirely trust the success, but he has the joy of facing the Rockies away from Coors Field next time out.
10. Foster Griffin (SP) - Foster Griffin got his comeuppance last time out with nine earned runs in 4 1/3 innings, but he still came away with a 3.53 ERA and 1.14 WHIP. With a respectable K/9 rate (8.7) and a penchant for weak contact, I think he's good enough to use in a two-start week, even with the bipolar matchups.

player_cards.blurb_en: A black sheep start against the Blue Jays on April 19 was followed by an unfair one in the thin air of Mexico City, inflating Ryne Nelson's ERA and cratering his roster rate. But he's looked great since then and has some particularly favorable matchups here against the bottom-ranked Giants and the Rockies away from Coors Field.

Walbert Urena excels at pitching to contact with a high ground-ball rate and basement-level exit velocity readings, and he also has some modest strikeout appeal thanks to a plus changeup. His outings have tended to run short, but he's in line for two of them week, with one being a particularly favorable matchup against the Rangers.

Reid Detmers continues to underperform his peripherals and has become an exhausting inclusion here week after week, but with two starts against strikeout-prone teams, you could hope for at least one quality start and upward of 15 strikeouts.

Fantasy Baseballers were by and large too quick to abandon Andrew Abbott when he got off to a poor start, and now he's sporting a 1.19 ERA in his past four. The Phillies offense has shown some signs of coming around, but it still ranks in the bottom half of the league.

Injuries have brought the Mets offense to a state of abject misery, ranking fourth-to-last in runs scored, and Cade Cavalli has already proven his mettle against them, striking out 10 in a quality start on April 29.

We're getting into more dangerous territory here, seeing as a pitch-to-contact fly-ball guy is inherently vulnerable at Fenway Park. But the Red Sox have fallen to second-to-last in runs scored, and Bailey Ober is only a start removed from a complete game shutout (otherwise known as, um, a shutout).

There may be no redeeming Zac Gallen in the long run, but in the short run, he lines up for two of the best matchups you could ask for, with the Giants ranking dead last in runs scored and the Rockies doing what they do away from Coors Field. In points leagues, he's a valid play.

The downside risk remains for Andrew Painter, but he did seem to be on the right track by fading his fastball in his latest start and ended up allowing just one run in five innings. The Reds and Guardians make for some nice matchups.

Eduardo Rodriguez got through his weekend start at Coors Field OK and had allowed one earned run over 15 1/3 innings in his previous two. I don't entirely trust the success, but he has the joy of facing the Rockies away from Coors Field next time out.

Foster Griffin got his comeuppance last time out with nine earned runs in 4 1/3 innings, but he still came away with a 3.53 ERA and 1.14 WHIP. With a respectable K/9 rate (8.7) and a penchant for weak contact, I think he's good enough to use in a two-start week, even with the bipolar matchups.
